content.text: What Are Root User Credentials?

When you create an AWS account, you establish a root user — the original, permanently-embedded identity tied to the account itself. Unlike IAM users and roles that are created and managed within an account, the root user exists at a deeper layer: it is the credential set used to create and manage the account envelope around everything else.

Root user credentials consist of two distinct authentication mechanisms that serve different purposes and carry very different risk profiles: the email/password combination for console access, and optionally root access keys for programmatic access. Understanding both — including when each can be used and why one must never exist — is fundamental to keeping an AWS account secure.
Credential Component Overview

attrs.alt: Root user credential components: email+password, MFA, access keys, storage — with security progression and MFA type comparison

content.text: The diagram maps all four credential dimensions for the root user: the email+password pair (highest risk if leaked, needs MFA to be safe), MFA devices (the critical mitigating control, up to 8 devices since 2023), root access keys (should never exist), and secure credential storage practices. The security progression at the bottom shows the path from a vulnerable new account to a properly locked root.
Email and Password Credential

The root user email is both the login username and the account contact address. It cannot be changed through normal account settings — changing it requires verifying control of the new address and is itself a root-only action. This permanence is by design: the email is a shared secret that proves ownership of the account.
Password Requirements

AWS enforces a minimum password policy for root user passwords: at least 8 characters, no maximum length beyond 128 characters. In practice, the root password should be much stronger than the minimum:
Use a randomly generated password of at least 20–30 characters
Include uppercase, lowercase, numbers, and symbols
Store it exclusively in a password manager — never in a text file, email, or shared document
Never reuse a password from any other service
Rotate annually or immediately if any breach is suspected

The root user email and password combination should be treated as equivalent to physical access to your data center. The same policies that control who can enter a server room should control who knows these credentials.
Changing the Root Password

To change the root password, you must be logged in as the root user (you cannot reset it via IAM). The process is under Account Settings → Security credentials → Password. If you are locked out, AWS provides a password recovery flow via email verification — another reason the root email must always be accessible.

# Verify which identity is currently logged in before performing root actions:
aws sts get-caller-identity

# Expected output for root (note: no UserId, Arn shows root):
# {
#   "UserId": "123456789012",
#   "Account": "123456789012",
#   "Arn": "arn:aws:iam::123456789012:root"
# }

# If Arn contains :root — you are logged in as root. Log out and use IAM instead.
Multi-Factor Authentication for the Root User

Enabling MFA on the root user is the single most important security action after account creation. A password alone can be phished, guessed, or leaked from a data breach on another service. MFA requires a second factor from a separate physical device or authentication platform — an attacker who obtains your password still cannot log in without the MFA factor.

AWS supports multiple MFA types for the root user. Since 2023, you can register up to 8 MFA devices on a single user — this is critical for the root user because losing your only MFA device means account lockout.
MFA Type Selection
MFA Type
How It Works
Phishing Resistant
Recommendation
Virtual MFA (TOTP)
App generates 6-digit time-based code (RFC 6238)
No — code can be captured in real-time phishing
Minimum acceptable; use hardware if possible
Hardware TOTP Token
Physical device generates 6-digit code (e.g., Gemalto token)
No — same vulnerability as virtual TOTP
Better than virtual; eliminates phone-based SIM-swap risk
FIDO2 Security Key
Physical USB/NFC key uses public-key cryptography (e.g., YubiKey)
Yes — bound to the AWS domain
Best for root; unphishable by design
Passkey (2024+)
Device biometric (Touch ID, Face ID) using WebAuthn
Yes — bound to the AWS domain
Best convenience + security; syncs via platform
Enabling MFA on the Root User (Console)
Log in to the AWS Management Console as the root user
Click the account name (top right) → Security credentials
Scroll to Multi-factor authentication (MFA) → Assign MFA device
Choose your MFA type, give the device a name (e.g., 'root-yubikey-primary')
For virtual MFA: scan the QR code with your authenticator app and enter two consecutive codes
For FIDO2/passkey: follow the browser WebAuthn flow (insert key or use biometric)
Confirm the device appears in the MFA device list
Repeat with a second device (backup) under a different name

Register at least two MFA devices on the root user — a primary (e.g., hardware security key) and a backup (e.g., virtual MFA on a separate device). This is the only safe approach because losing the sole MFA device requires contacting AWS Support to recover access, which can take days.
Verifying MFA Status Programmatically

# Check root MFA status (requires IAM read access from an IAM user/role)
aws iam get-account-summary --query 'SummaryMap.AccountMFAEnabled'
# Returns: 1 if root MFA is enabled, 0 if not

# List MFA devices associated with the root user
# (Note: root MFA devices appear with user name "root" — IAM API uses a special path)
aws iam list-virtual-mfa-devices --assignment-status Assigned   --query 'VirtualMFADevices[?User.Arn!=null] | [?contains(User.Arn, )]'
Root Access Keys — Never Create Them

Root access keys are long-lived programmatic credentials (an Access Key ID and Secret Access Key) that, when they exist, allow any code or script that holds them to make AWS API calls with full root authority — bypassing every IAM policy, SCP, and permission boundary in the account. They are the most dangerous credential type in AWS.
Unlike IAM user access keys, root access keys
cannot be scoped
with IAM policies,
are not subject to SCPs
from AWS Organizations, and
do not require MFA
for API calls. An attacker who obtains root access keys has unrestricted programmatic access to your entire account indefinitely — or until you delete the keys.
Checking Whether Root Access Keys Exist

# Get account credential report (includes root key status)
aws iam generate-credential-report
aws iam get-credential-report --query 'Content' --output text | base64 -d | grep root

# Key fields in the report for the root user:
# access_key_1_active: false  ← what you want
# access_key_2_active: false  ← what you want
# mfa_active: true             ← what you want

# Direct check via account summary
aws iam get-account-summary --query 'SummaryMap.{
  AccountAccessKeysPresent: AccountAccessKeysPresent,
  AccountMFAEnabled: AccountMFAEnabled
}'
# AccountAccessKeysPresent should be 0
Deleting Root Access Keys
If root access keys exist, delete them immediately. You cannot delete them via the CLI as an IAM user — you must log in as the root user in the console:
Sign in to the console as root
Click account name → Security credentials
Scroll to Access keys section
Click Delete next to any listed keys — confirm deletion
Re-run the credential report to confirm AccountAccessKeysPresent is 0

If your organization requires programmatic automation that currently uses root access keys, migrate to an IAM role with only the necessary permissions. For root-only tasks (like closing the account), use the console — no API-based automation should ever require root credentials.
Monitoring Root User Activity

Even though the root user should rarely be used, every action it takes must be audited. AWS CloudTrail logs all API calls and console actions, including root user logins and any operations performed as root.

The EventBridge rule above triggers whenever any root console login occurs. Connect it to an SNS topic that emails your security team. Any unexpected root login should trigger an immediate investigation and potential credential rotation.
Account Recovery — Regaining Root Access
There are three scenarios where you might be locked out of the root user, and each has a different recovery path:
Scenario
Recovery Method
Timeline
Forgot root password
Click 'Forgot password' on login page → reset via root email
Minutes
Lost access to root email
Update email via root console login (requires current password + MFA)
Requires current root access
Lost MFA device (still have password)
Use backup MFA device (if registered) or contact AWS Support
Hours to days via support
Lost password AND MFA device
Contact AWS Support with account verification documents
1–3 business days
Compromised account (attacker changed email)
Contact AWS Support with account creation evidence and payment proof
Days — outcome not guaranteed

The hardest recovery scenario is a compromised account where the attacker has changed the root email. AWS Support can help but the process is slow and uncertain. Preventing this by using MFA is far better than recovering from it.
The Role of Alternate Contacts in Recovery

AWS Support uses the account's registered contact information to verify identity during recovery. Keeping the account contacts current — especially the Security contact — ensures AWS reaches the right team when there is an incident. The alternate contacts are not used for root login but are critical for support-assisted recovery.
Root Credential Rotation Policy
Unlike IAM access keys (which have a clear rotation workflow), root user credentials have no AWS-native rotation reminder. Teams that handle AWS accounts should implement their own rotation schedule:
Root password: rotate annually, or immediately after any team member with knowledge of it leaves
MFA devices: review the device list semi-annually; remove devices belonging to departed staff
Root email: change only if the underlying mailbox becomes unmanageable; minimize changes
Document all changes in an internal security runbook with date and approver

# Script to verify root credential hygiene (run quarterly)
#!/bin/bash
echo "=== Root Credential Health Check ==="

# 1. Check root MFA
MFA=
echo "Root MFA enabled:  (should be 1)"

# 2. Check root access keys
KEYS=
echo "Root access keys present:  (should be 0)"

# 3. Check for recent root activity
echo "=== Root Activity (last 30 days) ==="
aws cloudtrail lookup-events   --lookup-attributes AttributeKey=Username,AttributeValue=root   --start-time 2026-06-01T00:56:32+00:00   --query 'Events[].{Time:EventTime, Event:EventName}'   --output table

echo "=== Check complete ==="
Security Best Practices Summary
Control
Priority
Action
Enable root MFA
Critical / Do now
Register 2+ devices; prefer FIDO2 or passkey
Delete root access keys
Critical / Do now
Console → Security credentials → Delete all keys
Set strong root password
Critical / Do now
20+ chars, password manager, never reuse
Monitor root logins
High
CloudTrail + EventBridge alert on root signin events
Register alternate contacts
High
Billing, Operations, Security contacts in Account settings
Annual credential review
Medium
Rotate password, audit MFA devices, update contacts
Document root credentials
Medium
Internal runbook: who holds them, last rotation date
Root User Session Behavior and Console Timeouts
Root user console sessions behave differently from IAM sessions in a few important ways:
Behavior
Root User
IAM User / Role
Session duration
No forced timeout — lasts until you sign out or browser closes
Configurable timeout; role sessions expire (1h default for assumed roles)
MFA requirement
Prompted at every login if MFA is enabled; no re-prompt mid-session
Can enforce MFA per-API-call via IAM condition keys
Session tokens
Root console sessions do not generate STS tokens for API use
AssumeRole generates time-limited STS tokens
Parallel sessions
One root console session at a time; multiple browser tabs share the session
Multiple concurrent IAM sessions are independent
Session policies
Cannot be scoped by session policies
STS sessions support inline session policies for privilege reduction

The inability to scope root sessions with session policies means there is no safe way to 'limit' a root login once it is established. Always log out immediately after completing a root-required task and close the browser tab.
AWS GuardDuty: Detecting Root Credential Abuse

AWS GuardDuty provides managed threat detection that monitors CloudTrail, VPC Flow Logs, and DNS logs for anomalous behavior. Several GuardDuty finding types specifically relate to root credential misuse:
GuardDuty Finding
What It Detects
Severity
Policy:IAMUser/RootCredentialUsage
Root user made an API call (any API call from root)
Medium
UnauthorizedAccess:IAMUser/ConsoleLoginSuccess.B
Successful console login from an unusual location
Medium
Recon:IAMUser/MaliciousIPCaller.Custom
API calls from a known-malicious IP (applies to root too)
High
CredentialAccess:IAMUser/AnomalousBehavior
Root behaves in unusual ways compared to baseline
High

Enable GuardDuty in every account and region. The Policy:IAMUser/RootCredentialUsage finding fires whenever root makes any API call — this is intentionally sensitive, because any root API usage is abnormal in a well-configured account. If you receive this finding and did not expect root activity, treat it as a potential compromise.

# Enable GuardDuty in a region (repeat for each region you use)
aws guardduty create-detector --enable --finding-publishing-frequency FIFTEEN_MINUTES

# List any existing GuardDuty detectors
aws guardduty list-detectors

# Retrieve recent findings filtered to root-related types
DETECTOR=$(aws guardduty list-detectors --query 'DetectorIds[0]' --output text)
aws guardduty list-findings --detector-id "$DETECTOR" \
  --finding-criteria '{"Criterion":{"type":{"Eq":["Policy:IAMUser/RootCredentialUsage"]}}}'
Root User and AWS Organizations

When your AWS account is part of an AWS Organization (as a member account), the root user's capabilities are constrained by the organization structure — but in a specific and sometimes surprising way:

Service Control Policies (SCPs) from the management account DO restrict what member account root users can do. An SCP that denies ec2:* also denies the member account root user from calling ec2:* API operations.
SCPs do NOT restrict the management account root user. The management account root has unrestricted organization-wide authority that no SCP can limit.
The management account root can invite and remove member accounts, enable organization services, and manage SCPs that affect all other accounts.
Member account root users can still perform root-only console actions (close account, change account email) even if SCPs restrict API calls.
This SCP constraint on member account roots is a
security feature
, not a limitation. It means you can use an SCP at the organization level to enforce that even a member account root user cannot create IAM access keys, for example:

Attach this SCP to the root of your organization or to the OU containing member accounts. It ensures that even if a member account root user attempts to create root access keys, the API call is denied by the organization layer.
Recognizing and Responding to Root Credential Compromise
If you suspect the root user credentials have been compromised, act in this priority order:
Immediately change the root user password from a clean, trusted device
Check Security credentials for any unrecognized MFA devices — remove them
Check IAM for any new users, roles, or access keys created recently
Review CloudTrail for all root activity in the last 90 days
Check for any S3 buckets made public, EC2 instances launched, or IAM policies modified
Contact AWS Support (phone) if you cannot regain control of the root account
Terminate any unauthorized resources that were created
Enable GuardDuty and Security Hub if not already active to aid forensic analysis

# Rapid forensic check: what did root do in the last 7 days?
aws cloudtrail lookup-events \
  --lookup-attributes AttributeKey=Username,AttributeValue=root \
  --start-time $(date -u -d '7 days ago' '+%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ') \
  --query 'Events[].{Time:EventTime, Event:EventName, Source:EventSource}' \
  --output table

# Check for newly created IAM users (look for anything you did not create)
aws iam list-users --query 'Users[].{Name:UserName, Created:CreateDate}' --output table

# Check for active root access keys
aws iam get-account-summary --query 'SummaryMap.AccountAccessKeysPresent' --output text

Speed matters in a credential compromise. Every minute the attacker retains access, they can create new backdoors (IAM users, lambda functions, cross-account roles). Changing the password and removing unauthorized MFA devices should happen within the first two minutes of detecting a compromise.
Root Credentials Checklist for New Accounts
Use this checklist immediately after creating a new AWS account — complete all items before doing any other work:
Step
Action
Verify With
Enable MFA on root user (register 2 devices)
IAM → Security credentials → MFA devices list
Delete root access keys if any exist
IAM get-account-summary → AccountAccessKeysPresent = 0
Set a strong root password (20+ chars, password manager)
Change password in Security credentials
Set alternate contacts (Billing, Operations, Security)
Account → Alternate contacts
Enable CloudTrail in all regions
CloudTrail → Trails → multi-region trail active
Enable GuardDuty
GuardDuty → Detector active
Create IAM admin user or SSO access for daily work
Verify you can log in as IAM admin without root
Log out as root, log in as IAM admin, confirm access works
aws sts get-caller-identity → shows IAM ARN, not root

After completing this checklist, the root credentials should be stored securely, known only to designated account custodians, and not used again until a root-only task arises. All subsequent work — creating resources, managing IAM, configuring services — happens through IAM identities, not root.
Root Credentials in Compliance and Audit Contexts

Several compliance frameworks and AWS security standards reference root user credential management explicitly. Understanding what auditors look for helps you satisfy these requirements from the start rather than retrofitting them:
Framework
Root Credential Requirement
How to Satisfy
AWS Foundational Security Best Practices (FSBP)
Root MFA must be enabled; root access keys must not exist
IAM.6 and IAM.4 controls in Security Hub
CIS AWS Foundations Benchmark
Sections 1.1-1.4: root MFA, no root access keys, no root console login activity
CloudTrail alerts + credential report
PCI DSS (cloud scope)
Shared credentials prohibited; MFA required for administrative access
Root must have MFA; no shared root password across teams
SOC 2 (CC6 series)
Logical access controls; privileged access monitoring
CloudTrail + GuardDuty findings for root activity

AWS Security Hub can automatically evaluate your account against FSBP and CIS controls. When you enable Security Hub, it continuously monitors the state of root MFA and the existence of root access keys and surfaces findings in its dashboard.

# Enable Security Hub in a region
aws securityhub enable-security-hub --enable-default-standards

# List Security Hub findings related to root user controls
aws securityhub get-findings \
  --filters '{"ComplianceSecurityControlId":[{"Value":"IAM.4","Comparison":"EQUALS"},{"Value":"IAM.6","Comparison":"EQUALS"}]}' \
  --query 'Findings[].{Title:Title,Status:Compliance.Status}'

IAM.4 checks that root access keys do not exist. IAM.6 checks that root MFA is enabled. Both should show PASSED in a properly configured account. If you join AWS Organizations and delegate a Security Hub administrator account, these findings roll up across all member accounts into a single compliance view.
Practical Scenario: What Happens Without Root MFA
Consider a realistic attack scenario to understand why root MFA is non-negotiable:
A developer creates an AWS account with the company email address aws-admin@company.com
The developer uses a weak root password that is also used for several other services
A breach at an unrelated service exposes this password in a credential dump
An attacker queries the dump, finds the email and password, and tries it on AWS console login
Without MFA, the attacker gains full root console access immediately
The attacker creates new IAM admin users, launches EC2 instances for cryptocurrency mining, and exfiltrates S3 data — all before the legitimate team notices
The attacker also creates root access keys, giving them persistent API access even if the password is later changed

With MFA enabled on the root user, step 5 fails. The attacker has the email and password but cannot complete the login without the second factor from a physical device they do not possess. MFA converts a credential breach from a catastrophic account takeover into a contained, recoverable incident.

This is not a hypothetical pattern. Cryptocurrency mining abuse and data exfiltration via compromised AWS credentials is a well-documented attack vector that AWS publishes guidance about in its security documentation. Root MFA is the single most impactful control to prevent this class of attack.
AWS has mechanisms to detect unusual root activity, but detection is not prevention. MFA prevents the attack entirely rather than detecting it after damage is done.
Summary

Root user credentials in AWS consist of an email/password pair for console access and optionally root access keys — which should never exist. The email and password are the account's identity anchor, and they must be secured with MFA before any other work proceeds. AWS supports up to 8 MFA devices per user; register at least two (a primary hardware key and a virtual backup) so that losing one device does not lock you out.
Root access keys bypass every security control in AWS and must be
deleted immediately

if they exist. All programmatic automation should use IAM roles with scoped permissions. Monitor root user activity via CloudTrail and alert on any root login with an EventBridge rule. Treat root credentials like physical access to a data center — known only to designated account owners, stored securely, rotated annually, and used only for the handful of tasks that literally require root authority.
